turns.content: I'm porting our old Python crypto library to Rust and I thought switching algorithms was as easy as swapping the cipher module, but my code compiles with an error saying SymmetricCipher trait is not implemented for my type. I assumed if I just imported a different crate it would automatically work like in Python where you change the function call.

You are likely confusing how Rust traits differ from dynamic dispatch or function overloading found in other languages. In the RustCrypto ecosystem, the SymmetricCipher trait is implemented by specific concrete types like Chacha20Poly1305 or AESGcmSiv, but there isn't a generic wrapper that handles all algorithms automatically without explicit type parameters. You need to explicitly implement your struct using the trait bound <T: SymmetricCipher> rather than assuming it will be available once you add the dependency.

Okay, so I can't just change which file I import from? It feels like I should have been able to do something similar to what we did with the serialization traits where different crates provided different implementations. Can you explain why this is more restrictive than how other languages handle this kind of abstraction?

The key difference is that Rust requires compile-time guarantees for security-critical components like crypto, so the SymmetricCipher trait cannot be implemented generically across different crates without explicit specialization. Unlike serialization traits where you might have a generic serde_json::Value or similar abstraction that adapts to many formats, crypto libraries in Rust require you to pin your algorithm choice at compile time to prevent accidental use of weak ciphers like DES which are now deprecated.

That makes sense for security but it's frustrating because we need to support multiple algorithms for different clients. Is there a way to create a generic wrapper that can hold any SymmetricCipher type without losing the trait bounds?

Yes, you can use generics with associated types or bound constraints. Instead of trying to make a blanket implementation, define your struct with a generic parameter T where T: SymmetricCipher + Default + Send + Sync. This forces the compiler to verify that whichever algorithm you choose actually implements the required trait before allowing it into your codebase.

Hey, I was just looking at the docs for the crypto trait crates and I thought since we're using generics with the SymmetricCipher trait, maybe I could just swap in a different cipher like ChaCha20 or AES-GCM without changing any of my existing code?

That's actually a really good instinct and it is exactly why these crates are so powerful. The idea is that you define your interface using the SymmetricCipher trait instead of hardcoding a specific algorithm, which lets you easily swap implementations later. However, just changing the generic parameter isn't enough on its own; you also need to make sure the concrete type implements all the required methods like encrypt and decrypt correctly.
Right, so I think I can just change my struct's field from a Box<ChaCha20> to a Box<AesGcm> because both implement the trait?

You're very close there. The generic type parameter in your code will definitely accept either ChaCha20Poly1305 or AesGcm if they both satisfy the SymmetricCipher trait bounds, but you need to check that the concrete crate actually exports those types with the correct feature flags enabled.
Oh wow, I didn't realize we had to enable specific features for the different cipher suites. So if I want AES-GCM instead of ChaCha20, do I just add a new dependency?

Not necessarily a whole new dependency. The rust-crypto crate is designed to be modular so you can enable specific features like aes-gcm or chacha20-poly1305 within the same crate, which keeps your project dependencies lean and avoids bloat.

That makes sense. But what about the derive macros? I saw some code using #[derive] on a struct that holds the cipher, but I'm not sure how that interacts with the trait bounds or if it's even necessary here?

You're asking a great question. In many cases, you don't need to derive any macros on your wrapper struct as long as you are explicitly constructing the cipher object using the newtype pattern or just holding it in an enum variant. The trait bounds are checked at compile time when you try to call methods like encrypt(). You should avoid deriving traits directly on your own structs unless they implement specific security properties that require zero-copy behavior.

Okay, so I think my plan was just to change the field type and add a feature flag. But what if I want to test both algorithms in my application without having to write separate code paths for each one?

That's where the enum pattern shines. You can define an Enum with variants like CipherType::ChaCha20 and CipherType::AesGcm, then use a generic function that takes a reference to this enum and dispatches based on which variant is selected using match statements.
